claims.claim: Under overcharging conditions, the safety vent of the 86 Ah LFP battery opens at 1551 s when the surface temperature is 48.5°C, and a large amount of liquid electrolyte is emitted.
claims.quote: for the overcharging condition, the safety vent opens at 1551 s, when the surface temperature of the battery is 48.5 ◦C. The safety vent opens for only 1 s, and a large amount of liquid is emitted.

claims.claim: Under overheating conditions, the safety vent of the 86 Ah LFP battery opens at 842 s when the surface temperature is 94°C, releasing a large amount of white smoke over some time.
claims.quote: For the overheating condition, the safety vent opens at 842 s when the battery surface temperature is 94 ◦C. The safety vent opens, releasing a large amount of white smoke over some time.

claims.claim: Safety venting under overheating is primarily caused by electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting under overcharging is primarily caused by gas release inside the battery.
claims.quote: The predominant reason for the safety venting of the LFP battery is the electrolyte volatilization under overheating... However, for the overcharging condition, when the safety vent opens, the surface temperature is 48.5 ◦C, and the electrolyte ejects as a liquid. Overcharging promotes gas production inside the battery, which also shows that the main reason for the safety venting is caused by the gas release of the LFP battery.

claims.claim: The total gas volume released during thermal runaway of the 86 Ah LFP battery is 62.1 L under overcharging and 101.3 L under overheating, with CO2 and H2 accounting for the majority in both cases.
claims.quote: the total gas generations are 62.1 and 101.3 L under overcharging and overheating... CO2 and H2 account for the majority of the released gases

claims.claim: The XFED peak value under overheating (11.6) is higher than under overcharging (7.7), but XFED remains below 1 before thermal runaway under overheating, whereas under overcharging XFED exceeds 1 for 1211 s before thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: The peak value of XFED for the overheating condition is 11.6, which is much higher than the 7.7 for the overcharging condition... the XFED under overheating is less than 1 before TR... but the XFED of gas release is more than 1 under overcharging when TR occurs before 1211 s.

claims.claim: The peak mass loss rates under overheating (−4.3 and −5.7 g/s) are substantially higher than under overcharging (−0.53 and −1.84 g/s), and the average mass loss rate under overheating (2.25 g/s) is much higher than under overcharging (0.24 g/s).
claims.quote: The two peak mass loss rates are (cid:0) 0.53 and (cid:0) 1.84 g/s under overcharging, but the peak mass loss rates are (cid:0) 4.3 and (cid:0) 5.7 g/s under overheating... The average mass loss rates are 0.24 and 2.25 g/s under overcharging and overheating.

claims.claim: The peak surface temperature during thermal runaway is 423°C under overcharging and 372.1°C under overheating for the 86 Ah LFP battery, while the peak temperature of released gas is nearly equal at 342.4°C and 343.4°C respectively.
claims.quote: The peak surface temperature of the battery during TR under overcharging and overheating is 423 and 372.1 ◦C, respectively, and the peak temperature of gas release is 342.4 and 343.4 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: The onset of self-heating in the study NCA 21700 cell was detected at 86 ± 1 °C at 100% SoC, more than 35 hours before thermal runaway occurred.
claims.quote: We find that the study cell can self-heat for over 35 h before thermal runaway. This leaves more than a full day from the onset of detectable failure signals during which time mitigation strategies may be employed.

claims.claim: The maximum heating rate during thermal runaway increases exponentially with state of charge, consistent with reports on NMC cells.
claims.quote: We observe that the maximum heating rate increases exponentially with SoC, as shown in Fig. 4b, which is an agreement with other reports on NMC cells [47].

claims.claim: The venting temperature does not depend on state of charge and typically occurs around 180 °C during heat ramp tests, approximately 60 °C higher than during HWS testing.
claims.quote: The venting temperature does not appear to be dependent on the SoC, and typically occurs around 180 °C. We note that the vent temperature here is around 60 °C higher than that observed during HWS testing.

claims.claim: Mass loss during thermal runaway shows a reasonably linear dependence on state of charge, with higher SoC cells losing more mass.
claims.quote: Fig. 4d demonstrates that more cell mass is lost at higher SoC, with a reasonably linear correlation.

claims.claim: The energy transferred to heating the cell during thermal runaway is approximately 31 kJ, which is only about 50% of the total stored cell energy of approximately 60 kJ, with the residual energy attributed to ejecta.
claims.quote: The resulting distribution, shown in Fig. 5c, is centred around 31 kJ. Importantly, this is only about 50 % of the total energy stored by the cell, which is around 60 kJ. The residual energy not accounted for, around 30 kJ, is most likely attributable to the thermal runaway ejecta, both as thermal and kinetic energy.

claims.claim: The trigger method affects gas production rate, mass loss, and maximum temperature of a Li-ion cell as much as its state of charge.
claims.quote: It is concluded that the trigger method affects the gas production rate, mass loss, and maximum temperature of the cell as much as its state of charge.

claims.claim: Specific total gas production increases linearly with SOC, ranging from 0.35 L/Ah at 25% SOC to approximately 2.1 L/Ah at 100% SOC for a 157 Ah NMC811 prismatic cell.
claims.quote: the specific total gas production went from 0.35 L/Ah to 2.1 L/Ah in the range 25–100 % SOC

claims.claim: TR onset temperature decreases by 18°C per 25% increase in SOC for the tested NMC811 large format prismatic cell.
claims.quote: TR onset temperature had a decrease of 18°C per 25 % SOC increase

claims.claim: Maximum cell surface temperature during thermal runaway increases by approximately 90°C per 25% SOC increase.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature increased approximately 90°C per 25 % SOC increase

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, mass loss varied from 35% to 75% depending on the TR trigger method, while the average mass loss increase from 25% to 100% SOC was 35 percentage points.
claims.quote: the mass loss at 100 % SOC varied from 35 % to 75 % with different TR trigger methods, while the average increase for 25–100 % SOC was 35 percentage points
